Maladaptive Strategies.value: Being in circumstances that remind me of the event (eg, being in the dark).
I gamble to distract myself or feel excitement

Threat.value: I frequently look behind me to check if someone is following me
I only feel safe in a very limited number of places
I have difficulty falling asleep because I need to stay alert

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
Remembering the event brings back the same terror I felt at the time
Something in my surroundings can instantly bring back the full memory without warning

Negative Appraisals.value: I am no longer capable of doing the things that defined me
The event happened because of the way I acted

issues.item: Lost Community: I have lost my connection to my community and culture
issues.explanation: This is somewhat inconsistent with the provided demographics/context. The patient is a 45-year-old single shopowner with military trauma; a strong loss-of-community/culture appraisal is possible, but this specific item suggests a broad sociocultural displacement that is not clearly supported by the rest of the profile. It stands out as less coherent than the other appraisals, which are more directly PTSD-related.

issues.item: Relationship Reminders: When people say things like my ex
issues.explanation: This trigger is hard to reconcile with the rest of the profile. The trauma type is military, and no relationship/interpersonal trauma is indicated. A breakup-related cue can occur in PTSD, but as a primary trigger here it is not well aligned with the trauma context or the rest of the formulation.

vignette:
Khalid is a 45-year-old Middle Eastern man who owns a small shop and has a PTSD score of 67. He describes living on edge in the market streets around his shop, where crowds, children crying, and the smell of alcohol can suddenly hit him and throw him back into the worst moment of his military trauma. He recalls a chaotic incident in which he was trapped in a crowded area under threat, with shouting around him, children crying nearby, and the sharp smell of alcohol in the air; at the peak of danger he could not move freely or feel sure who was safe, and he remembers that he did not act decisively enough to protect others or himself.

When Khalid talks about the event, he does not tell it as a smooth story. He breaks it into pictures: a face, a narrow space, a flash of movement, the look of fear. He says that remembering it brings the same terror back into his body, and that ordinary surroundings can drop him straight into the full scene without warning. At times he stops mid-sentence, scans the room, and seems pulled back by one image at a time rather than by a complete narrative.

His central belief is blunt and self-condemning: “I am not the man I was, I cannot do what I used to do, and I failed because of how I acted.” He also speaks in a flat, cut-off way, saying that he feels dead inside. The trauma has left him convinced that his own actions at the time showed weakness and that he is no longer capable of the kind of responsibility that once defined him.

In daily life, Khalid checks behind him repeatedly when he is out near his shop or walking home, and he limits himself to only a few places where he feels at ease. He avoids being in darkness or in situations that resemble the military incident. He has also stopped exercising, and he uses gambling to distract himself or chase a brief sense of excitement. Sleep is difficult because he feels he has to stay alert.

The crowded street outside his shop, a child crying in a nearby store, or the smell of alcohol from a customer can all bring the memory sharply forward. Once that happens, he becomes more watchful, looks over his shoulder, and narrows his world to the few places he considers safe. The more he judges himself as incapable and to blame, the more he scans for danger and the more easily ordinary cues around the shop set off the same image, terror, and sense of threat. This pattern has made him less available to customers and more withdrawn from the social life that usually sustains a shopowner in his community.
